core_meaning: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> nominalizes verbs and adjectives, turning them into abstract 'things,' 'facts,' or 'matters.' Unlike <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>, which refers to concrete physical objects, <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> refers to intangible things — situations, acts, experiences, and concepts.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>のこと、まだ<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きなの？
sentences.notes: <ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>のこと means 'the matter of [person]' or 'things about [person]' — a very natural way to talk about someone in an emotionally involved sense.

sentences.jp: <ruby>日<rt>に</rt></ruby><ruby>本<rt>ほん</rt></ruby><ruby>語<rt>ご</rt></ruby>を<ruby>毎<rt>まい</rt></ruby><ruby>日<rt>にち</rt></ruby><ruby>練<rt>れん</rt></ruby><ruby>習<rt>しゅう</rt></ruby>することが<ruby>大<rt>たい</rt></ruby><ruby>切<rt>せつ</rt></ruby>だよ。
sentences.translation: Practicing Japanese every day is important.
sentences.notes: 〜することが shows the classic grammatical use of こと nominalizing a verb phrase to make it the subject of a sentence.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>嘘<rt>うそ</rt></ruby>をついていたことに、ずっと<ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>づかなかった。
sentences.translation: I had no idea for a long time that he had been lying.
sentences.notes: 〜たことに nominalizes a past event to make it the target of a perception or emotion verb like <ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>づく.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>機<rt>き</rt></ruby><ruby>械<rt>かい</rt></ruby>は<ruby>絶<rt>ぜっ</rt></ruby><ruby>対<rt>たい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>触<rt>さわ</rt></ruby>れないこと。
sentences.translation: Do not touch this machine under any circumstances.
sentences.notes: 〜ないこと as a sentence-final instruction is a written register pattern used in rules, signs, and notices — it functions as a firm prohibition.

sentences.jp: <ruby>富<rt>ふじ</rt></ruby><ruby>士<rt>じ</rt></ruby><ruby>山<rt>さん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>ったことがある。
sentences.notes: 〜たことがある is one of the most common grammatical patterns using こと, expressing that one has had the experience of doing something.

dont_use: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> should not replace <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> when referring to actual physical items — saying <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べることが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>き is fine (the act of eating), but for 'I like this thing/dish' you'd say <ruby>これ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>き or use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>. Don't confuse the abstract nominalizer こと with the suffix ごと (<ruby>事<rt>ごと</rt></ruby> as in <ruby>仕<rt>し</rt></ruby><ruby>事<rt>ごと</rt></ruby>), which is a different reading entirely. Also avoid overusing こと as a filler nominalizer in casual speech where a simpler direct form sounds more natural.

confused_with.contrast: A common learner mistake is writing <ruby>机<rt>つくえ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>上<rt>うえ</rt></ruby>にあることを<ruby>取<rt>と</rt></ruby>って — using こと for a physical object on a desk, which sounds wrong because こと is abstract. The natural word here is <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>: <ruby>机<rt>つくえ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>上<rt>うえ</rt></ruby>にある<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>取<rt>と</rt></ruby>って. Use こと for intangible acts, facts, and situations; use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> for things you can see and touch.

frequency: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> is extremely common across all registers — spoken, written, formal, and casual. It appears constantly as both a standalone noun meaning 'matter/thing' and as a grammatical nominalizer. It is one of the highest-frequency words in Japanese overall.
anki_hint: こと turns verbs and abstract ideas into nouns — if you can't touch it, it's probably こと, not <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.
